why: why is my property read only
why doesn't property show up in loop
how: how to make property read only
how to hide property from loop
output: cannot assign to read only property

1_beginner_explanation.summary: When you create an object property like `user.name = 'Alice'`, JavaScript secretly attaches a settings menu to that property called a **Property Descriptor**. This menu has three main switches: `writable` (can it be changed?), `enumerable` (does it show up in loops?), and `configurable` (can it be deleted or have its settings changed?). By default, all are set to true, but you can change them using `Object.defineProperty()`.
1_beginner_explanation.analogy: Imagine a file on your computer. You can right-click it and check 'Read-Only' or 'Hidden'. Property Descriptors are the exact same thing, but for individual properties inside a JavaScript object.

2_internal_mechanism.execution_flow: When executing `Object.defineProperty(obj, key, descriptor)`, the engine modifies the internal Property Record in the Heap. If `writable: false`, any standard assignment (`=`) is silently ignored (or throws a TypeError in Strict Mode). If `enumerable: false`, the key is skipped during `for...in` and `Object.keys()` operations.
2_internal_mechanism.memory: Descriptors themselves take up a tiny bit of hidden memory for every single property on every object.

3_real_world_example.use_case: Creating a secure, read-only ID on an object that cannot be accidentally changed or deleted.

4_common_confusion.trap: Thinking `configurable: false` means the value can't be changed. `configurable` prevents the *deletion* of the property and prevents changing the *descriptor rules* themselves. It is `writable` that prevents changing the actual value.
4_common_confusion.fix: Writable = locks the VALUE. Configurable = locks the DELETION/RULES.

6_edge_case.quirk: If you define a property with `Object.defineProperty` but don't specify the flags, they default to `false`! This is the opposite of standard assignment where they default to `true`.
